story.text:
This is a XX-year-old XXX living in XXX.


One thing that made him happy recently was XXXX.

This is a XX-year-old XXX living in XXX.


One thing that made him happy recently was XXXX.

They are two of the more than 10,000 people who took part in a research project that tracked happy experiences. In total, the researchers gathered more than 100,000 happy moments.


Using these experiences, I made a map. A happy map.
On the left side are experiences that…

citation: Data: Akari Asai, Sara Evensen, Behzad Golshan, Alon Halevy, Vivian Li, Andrei Lopatenko, Daniela Stepanov, Yoshihiko Suhara, Wang-Chiew Tan, Yinzhan Xu, "HappyDB: A Corpus of 100,000 Crowdsourced Happy Moments", LREC '18, May 2018. | Paper | Data
info:
In 2017, more than 10,000 people tracked experiences that made them happy as part of a research project. The researchers gathered more than 100,000 happy moments.

Data methodology

I initially used the gemini-2.0-flash-001 LLM model to determine how much an agency score and time score (immediate vs. long-term), as well as a brief summary of the happy moment. These results helped me create a three-level topic taxonomy that are analogous to continents, countries, and states; there are obviously many ways to group these moments, so this is reflective of how I think about happy moments. I then prompted gemini-2.0-flash-001 to categorize each happy moment into the three-level taxonomy. I cleaned up the results in Open Refine to ensure there is no cross-taxonomy categorization; for example, the LLM sometimes put a moment into a state that is not within the proper parent country or continent. (This error is akin to saying that a moment belongs in New York state, and in the country of France.) From there, I used gemini-2.0-flash-001 to give each moment a score of how well it fits into its assigned categories, since sometimes moments didn't have a perfect category in the taxonomy; what you are more likely to see on the screen on the best fits.

Map methodology

The map is initially split up into 200,000 regions using a voronoi algorithm; it's looks like a giraffe's fur. From there, the top-level category—the "continents"—are initially given a starting atom, based on the average agency and time scores. It is then allowed to take over atoms based on the countries, states, and cities (moments) that it needs to fit within its bounds. That conquering process determined what the map looks like. This ultimately means that the moments mostly follow the x- and y-axis of time and agency, like a scatterplot. But since two things can't occupy the same space, it's not a perfect scatterplot. The moments are then placed on the map with illustrations of people. The people were drawn on paper with an ink pen and fountain pen, then digitized so they could be used as icons.

Data citation

Akari Asai, Sara Evensen, Behzad Golshan, Alon Halevy, Vivian Li, Andrei Lopatenko, Daniela Stepanov, Yoshihiko Suhara, Wang-Chiew Tan, Yinzhan Xu, "HappyDB: A Corpus of 100,000 Crowdsourced Happy Moments", LREC '18, May 2018. Paper | Data
